\oo '
LA|V OFf|CES
BROWN, HARPER, BURNS & HENTSCHKE
r27m EGH LUfr Oelvr srlE 2l)
SAN DIEGO. CALIFORNA 92]30
(619.561915
t^}254@2
July 3O, 199O
Fair Political Practices Commission
Legal Department
P.O. 8ox 807
Sacramento, CA 95804
Attn: Scott Hallabrin
Re: Request for Advice Regarding Participation by
Council/Redevelopment Agency Member Jack Moore on
Redevelopment Decisions
Dear Mr. Hallabrin:
The purpose of this letter is to request advice regarding the participation by Jack
Moore, Council and Redevelopment Agency Member in upcoming City Council and
Redevelopment Agency decisions pertaining to the implementation of the
Redevelopment Plan for the Solana Beach Redevelopment Project Area. This request
is made pursuant to Government Code Section 83114(b) and 2 Cal. Code of Regs.
Section 18329. Council/Agency Member Moore, upon my advice, has previously
abstained from participating in recent decisions regarding the adoption of the
Redevelopment Plan. The abstention was due to the lack of precise determination of
whether a disqualifying conflict existed, rather than on the ground of an
actual conflict. Mr. Moore has requested that I obtain an advise letter regarding future
implementation of the plan. The precise questions to be answered are (1 ) may Mr.
Moore participate in decisions applicable to the Redevelopment Project Area generally
such as approval of agreements with taxing agencies, approval of a specific plan for
design of the commercial corridor and other general matters? (2) May Mr. Moore
participate in specific proiect decisions when the proiect is in the Redevelopment
Project Area but more than 300 feet from Mr. Moore's property? (3) Was it necessary
for Mr. Moore to abstain from participating in the approval of the Redevelopment
Plan ?
The facts of this situation are summarized as follows. Solana Beach is a small
coastal city in San Diego County. lts population is less than 15,000 persons and it
is less than 10 square miles in area. The City is approximately 98 percent developed.
The City Council and Solana Beach Redevelopment Agency (which are the same
persons) have adopted a redevelopment plan for the Solana Beach Redevelopment


BROWN, HARPER. BURNS & HENISCH(E
Fair Political Practices Commission
Re: Request for Advise Letter
Page 2
July 30, 199O
Project Area. The Project Area is generally described as the commercial and industrial
corridor of the City along Highway 101 and Cedros Avenue, however, the boundaries
extend up Lomas Santa Fe Drive within the boundaries of the street right-of-way to
the Earl Warren Junior High School property and take in the Earl Warren Junior High
School property and industrial property adjoining the Ea.l Warren Junior High School
site. A map ofthearea is enclosed for your reference. Jack Moore is a Solana Beach
City Council member and a member ofthe Redevelopment Agency. Mr. Moore owns
a residence located approximately 250 feet from the right-of-way boundary of Lomas
Santa Fe Drive, lt is located between 3OO and 2500 feet from the primary areas of
the Redevelopment Project area i.e. the commercial/industrialarea along the Highway
101 corridor and the commercial/industrial area adjoining the Earl Warren Junior High
School site. The Redevelopment Plan does not propose any improvements to Lomas
Santa Fe Drive as a result of the plan except for the replacement of an asphalt curb,
gutter, and sidewalk with a concrete curb, gutter and sidewalk along a portion of
Lomas Santa Fe Drive. The project area contains no property designated for
residential use by the City General Plan or applicable zoning but is surrounded by
residential uses and properties. with the exception of certain portions of the project
area along Lomas Santa Fe Drive and Stevens Avenue which adjoin existing
commercial uses, Mr. Moore's residence is located off a short cul-de-sac street which
intersects with Granados Avenue which, in turn, intersects with Lomas Santa Fe
Orive. The property does not adjoin Lomas Santa Fe Drive but sits on a small bluff
overlooking a vacant piece of property which abuts Lomas Santa Fe Drive and
Granados Avenue. This vacant property is not within the project area and is
designated for residential use by the City General Plan and zoning. The purposes of
the redevelopment plan are to eliminate blight in the Project Area and in particular the
commercial corridor along Highway 101 and Cedros Avenue; revitalize the commercial
areas; and to implement the City housing element by providing lunding and programs
to provide housing for very low, low and moderate income persons and families.
ln order to assist in your advise preparation I have set forth below my analysis
regarding Mr. Moore's participation in the decision regarding plan adoption.
Government Code Section 87100 states:
"No public official at any level of state or local government
shall make, participate in making, or in any way attempt to
use his official position to influence a governmental
decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has
a financial interest."


L w o6Flc€S
BROWN, HARPER, BURNS & HENTSCHKE
Fair Political Practices Commission
Re: Request for Advise Letter
July
Government Code Section 871 03 states in part:
"An official has a financial interest in a decision within the
meaning of Government Code Section 87100 if it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financialeffect, distinguishable from its effect on the public
generally, on the official or a member of his or her
immediate family or on: . . . (b) any real property in which
the public official has a direct or indirect interest of
$1,OOO.OO. . . ."
Title 2, Section 18702.3 of the California Code of Regulations states in part:
"The effect of a decision is material as to real property in
which an official has a direct, indirect, or beneficial
ownership interest (not including a leasehold interest) if any
of the following applies: (1) the real property in which the
official has an interest, or any part of that real property, is
located within a 3OO foot radius of the boundaries (or the
proposed boundaries) of the property which is the sublect
of the decision, unless the decision will have no financial
effect upon the officials real property interest. . . ."
Section 18702.3 also provides:
"For the purpose of this Section 'the boundaries (or
proposed boundaries) of the property which is the subject
of the decision'or the boundaries (or proposed boundaries)
of the redevelopment project area whenever the decision is
a redevelopment decision to designate the survey area, to
make findings of blight, to select the project area, to adopt
the preliminary plan, to form a prolect area committee, to
certify the environmental document, to adopt a
redevelopment plan, to add territory to the redevelopment
atea, u to rescind or amend any of the above decisions."
Title 2, Section 18703.1 of the California Code of Regulations provides that the
effect of a governmental decision on a principal residence of a public official is not
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally where all of the following
conditions are met (a) the population of the jurisdiction is 25,OOO or less and the
Page 3
30, 1990


BROWN, HARPER, BURNS & HENTSCHKE
Fair Political Practices Commission
Re: Request for Advise Letter
Page 4
July 30, 199O
jurisdiction has geographic area of 1O square miles or less; (b) the olficial is required
to reside within the lurisdiction; (c) the official if an elected orficer has been elected
in an at large election; (dlthe decision does not have a direct effect on the officials
principal residence; (e) the residence is more than 300 feet from the boundaries of the
property which is the subject of the decision; (f) the principal residence is located on
a parcel of land one-quarter acre or less in size or a residential lot not larger than
125% of the medium residential lot size for the jurisdiction. That regulation also
provides that even if the above conditions are not met the effect of a governmental
decision on the principal residence of a public official may be determined to be not
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally in accordance with any other
regulations of the Fair Polilical Practices Commission not applicable solely to small
jurisdictions.
According to Title 2, Section 18703 of the California Code of Regulations a
material financial effect of a governmental decision on an official's interest is
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally unless the decision will effect
the official's interest in substantially the same manner as it will effect all members of
the public or a significant segment of the public.
Under the provisions of Section 18702.3 quoted previously there is essentially
a presumption that the decision to certify the environmental impact report and
approve the redevelopment plan will have a material financial effect on your residence
since it is located within 300 feet of the boundaries of the property which is the
subject of the decision. This presumption of material effect can be overcome only by
a showing that the decision will have no financial effect upon your residential real
property interest. Two expert real property appraisers have informally indicated that
the impact on Mr. Moore's residence from the approval of the redevelopment plan
would be diminimus and extremely difficult to measure. They also indicated that if
they were appraising his property they would likely not make any adjustments to value
based upon the facts of this particular circumstance. However, a formal appraisal of
the property or a formal written statement by an appraiser that the decision to adopt
the plan would have no financial impact has not been obtained, Thus, Jor the
purposes of my prior advice, I assumed that the decision to adopt the redevelopment
plan could have some diminimus impact on Mr. Moore's residential property and thus,
applying Section 1A7O2.3, I assumed that the effecr of the adoption of the
redevelopment plan is material as to his residential property.
The next question is whether the effect of the decision is distinguishable or not
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally. As mentioned previously,
Section 18703.1 of the Regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission


BROWN, HAX}ER. BURNS & HENTSCHKE
Fair Political Practices Commission
Re: Request for Advise Letter
Page 5
July 30, l99O
established a special regulation to help decide whether a governmental decision on a
principal residence of a public official is not dislinguishable from the effect on the
public generally for small lurisdictions. Unlortunately, becausehis principal residence
is located within 3OO feet of the boundaries of the property which is the subject of
the decision this regulation is inapplicable. We must then turn to other regulations or
laws relating to the determination of whether eftect is distinguishable from the effect
on the public generally. ln this case we would turn to Section 18703 of the
Regulations. Under that Section "a material ,inancial erfect of a governmental
decision (which we have presumed in this circumstance) is distinguishable from the
effect on the public generally unless the decision will eifect the officials interest in
substantially manner as it will effect all members of the public or a significant segment
ot the public." The question then boils down 10 whether the decision to approve the
redevelopment plan will have essentially the same impact on Mr. Moore's property as
it has on other residential real property in the city.
The relevant inquiry here is what constitutes a significant segment of the
public. As mentioned previously, the redevelopment project area is essentially
surrounded by residential properties. ll a 300 foot line were drawn around the
exterior boundaries of the project area much of the land within that 300 foot radius
except along the portion of the project area bounded by Stevens Avenue would be
residential property and most of that residential property would currently be developed
to its highest and best use. lt appears that the facts of this particular circumstance
are similar to those which were addressed in a formal opinion of the Fair Political
Practices Commission in the matter of the opinion requested by William L. Owen.
2 FPPC Opinions 77 (1976). ln that opinion the Fair Political Practices Commission
in determining whether a planning commissioner could who owned a home across the
street from one boundary of a "core area" which was the subject of significant
planning and redevelopment decisions stated;
"We conclude that residential homeowners within and in
the immediate vicinity of the 'core area' constitute a
"significant segment" of the public and that Planning
Commissioner Hunt accordingly need not disqualiiy himself
from participation merely because he owns a home as
described, While certain aspects of the plan may operate
to iocrease the value of this home, either as residential or
commercial property, it is apparent that the plan will have
a 'substantially similar' effect on numerous other residential
properties, perhaps throughout the entire city as well as
those near the 'core area', The effect of the plan on


tatvofFtc€s
BRO}r'N, HARPE& BURNS & HENTSCHKE
Fair Political Practices Commission
Re: Request for Advise Letter
Page 6
July 30, 199O
residential property values is speculative at this point. More importantly,
there is no indication that the plan or any portion thereof will have a
peculiar impact on the value of Commissioner Hunt's property. lf further
evidence emerges in the development of the plan which would
distinguish the effect of the plan or a portion of it upon Commissioner
Hunt's interest in comparison to other residential property owners, he
may be required to disqualify himself with respect to the matters before
him for decision." 2 FPPC Opinions 81 .
Whether the number of residential properties within the immediate vicinity of
the redevelopment pro.iect area constitutes a 'significant segment' of the public
generally is a factual decision which would need to be made based upon a comparison
of the number of residential properties within that immediate vicinity against the total
number of residences in the city. However, lthink that there is a reasonable
argument that in a city the size of Solana Beach essentially all residential properties
west of lnterstate 5 (please see the enclosed map) will have the same basic impact
from the redevelopment plan. The FPPC legal staff has taken the position that the
determination of whether there is a significant segment of the community affected by
the decision depends on whether the official "can show that all the properties roughly
the same distance away from the proposed proiect are similarly affected and that
these property owners constitute a significant segment of the population, or that the
effect on the remainder of the population will be substantially the same effect on
him." Wiczvnski Advise Letter A-90-162, April 23, 1990. P. 6. There is a question
as to whether underthe Wiczynski advise letter, all residential properties located west
of lnterstate 5 may be used in determining the relevant significant segment of the
public.
lf there is further information you need to respond to this request, please let me know.
Thank you for your attention to this matter.
DSH:maa
Enclosure
cc: Jack Moore

sections.question: The precise questions to be answered are (1) may Mr. Moore participate in decisions applicable to the Redevelopment Project Area generally such as approval of agreements with taxing agencies, approval of a specific plan for design of the commercial corridor and other general matters? (2) May Mr. Moore participate in specific project decisions when the project is in the Redevelopment Project Area but more than 300 feet from Mr. Moore's property? (3) Was it necessary for Mr. Moore to abstain from participating in the approval of the Redevelopment Plan?
sections.question_synthetic: Whether Council/Redevelopment Agency Member Jack Moore has a disqualifying financial conflict of interest that would prevent him from participating in decisions regarding the Solana Beach Redevelopment Project, including decisions about the redevelopment plan adoption and project-specific decisions more than 300 feet from his property.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request/letter seeking FPPC advice, not an FPPC response letter. No formal CONCLUSION or SHORT ANSWER section exists. The document is truncated at the end. The attorney has included substantial legal analysis in support of the request, citing relevant statutes and regulations. No FPPC conclusion/advice is provided in the visible text.

embedding.summary: This is an incoming request letter from an attorney at Brown, Harper, Burns & Hentschke to the FPPC's Legal Department requesting an advice letter regarding whether Jack Moore, a Solana Beach City Council and Redevelopment Agency member, has a disqualifying conflict of interest that would prohibit his participation in redevelopment decisions. The letter provides detailed factual background about Moore's residential property location relative to the redevelopment project area and includes legal analysis of Government Code sections 87100, 87103 and FPPC regulations 18702.3 and 18703.1.
